title: The concept of investment appraisal
summary: 9609 A Level — why businesses appraise capital projects, relevant cash flows, and qualitative factors.
learningObjectives: Explain the purpose of investment appraisal.
Outline qualitative factors in investment decisions.

simpleExplanation.title: Should we invest?
simpleExplanation.summary: Investment appraisal compares the costs and benefits of major projects to decide whether they earn enough return and fit the business strategy.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Buying a factory machine is like choosing a university course — high upfront cost, long-term payoff, hard to reverse. You would not decide on gut feel alone; you compare future benefits against cost, risk, and what you give up by not choosing alternatives.
steps: Capital projects involve large, long-term spending.
Use incremental cash flows — only costs and revenues that change.
Ignore sunk costs — money already spent cannot be recovered.
Weigh qualitative factors: strategy, brand, CSR, risk.

flashcards.back: To assess whether a capital project is financially worthwhile and supports business objectives.

flashcards.front: What is a relevant (incremental) cash flow?
flashcards.back: A future cash inflow or outflow that changes because the project goes ahead.
Past expenditure that cannot be recovered — excluded from appraisal.
Capital: long-term assets (machinery). Revenue: day-to-day operating costs (wages, materials).
Large, irreversible spending; forecasts of future cash flows may be wrong.
Brand image, employee morale, environmental impact, strategic fit, market entry.
Return forgone from the next best alternative use of capital.
Appraisal checks whether projects support long-term direction, not just short-term profit.

sections.content: **Investment appraisal** (10.3.1) opens **A Level Topic 10.3**. Paper 3/4 case questions often present **two projects** and ask you to appraise then **recommend**. Start by identifying **relevant cash flows** — examiners penalise including sunk costs or ignoring strategic context.

Capital investment — new machinery, stores, IT systems, acquisitions — ties up large sums for years. Appraisal helps directors decide whether expected returns justify **risk**, **financing cost**, and **opportunity cost** (capital used here cannot be used elsewhere).

Methods in 10.3.2–10.3.4 quantify returns; this topic sets **what to include** in the analysis.

items: **Include:** incremental revenues, incremental costs, initial outlay, residual/scrap values, working capital changes.
**Exclude:** sunk costs, allocated overheads that do not change, non-incremental items.
**Financing costs:** usually handled via the discount rate (NPV), not as a separate cash flow.

sections.question: A firm spent $50 000 last year on a feasibility study for a new warehouse. It now considers building the warehouse for $400 000 with forecast net cash inflows of $120 000 per year. Should the $50 000 study cost be included in appraisal?
sections.solution:
**No** — the $50 000 is a **sunk cost**. It is already spent whether or not the warehouse proceeds.

Relevant flows: **initial $400 000 outlay** and **incremental $120 000 annual net cash inflows** (plus any terminal value). The feasibility study is useful background but not part of the calculation.

sections.content: Not all impacts appear in cash flows: **brand reputation**, **employee skills**, **CSR/environment**, **competitive pre-emption**, **government relations**. A project with borderline NPV may still be accepted for strategic reasons — or rejected despite positive NPV if reputational risk is high.
In **discuss** questions, write a short **quantitative summary** (e.g. "Project A NPV positive") then a **qualitative paragraph** before your recommendation — examiners reward structure.

sections.label: Mark an investment appraisal concept question
